attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: AI-assisted first-pass catalogue entry; for maintainer + Tarot History Forum review.

name: Mamluk Playing Cards — Mulūk wa-nuwwāb (the Direct Ancestor)
description:
The single most important upstream object in this repo: the four-suit pack Europe copied. The surviving deck — *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb* ("Kings and Deputies"), the game called *kanjifah* — is a Mamluk-era set (Egypt & Syria, dated to roughly the 15th century, c. 1500), of which **roughly 48 cards survive — about 43 from the original pack plus a handful of cruder later replacement cards** (the original deck had 52), held at the **Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul** (found and published by L. A. Mayer, 1939/1971).

It has **four suits** — cups, coins, swords, and **polo-sticks** — each running **Ace–10 plus three court cards** (a king and two deputies). The courts are **aniconic**: no human figures, only abstract design and calligraphic inscriptions naming the rank. When this pack reached Iberia and Italy in the **1370s** (as *naipes* / *naibi*), Italians later added a fifth suit of 21 triumphs + the Fool — and that addition, in the **1440s**, is tarot. So tarot is *this deck plus trumps*. The polo-sticks, a game Europeans didn't play, were redrawn as **batons/wands**; cups, coins and swords carried over directly.

**How the suits became European:** Cups → Cups, Coins → Coins, Swords → Swords (Latin suits), and Polo-sticks → Batons. The Latin-suited packs (Italian/Spanish) preserve the Mamluk shapes most faithfully; the later French suits (hearts/tiles/clovers/pikes) are a simplification of these.

**China → Islam → Europe:** Playing cards most likely begin in Tang-dynasty China (money-suited cards). Through an undocumented transmission they reach the Islamic world as *kanjifah*; the Mamluk pack is the form that crossed into Iberia and Italy in the 1370s. Italy's fifth suit of triumphs (1440s) makes it tarot. The deepest roots are gaming, not divination.

GAME OR DIVINATION? A gaming/gambling object. The deepest roots of tarot are a card-game tradition (Tang-China money-suited cards → Mamluk *kanjifah* → European *naibi*); divination cultures existed alongside but used *other* instruments.

ANCESTRAL STATUS: **direct ancestor** (the parent of the European four-suit pack), as distinct from the *cousin* branches (e.g. Ganjifa) that did not become tarot.

SOURCES: L. A. Mayer, *Mamluk Playing Cards* (1971); Michael Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980); Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul.

---
*AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

**IMAGE PROVENANCE / TODO (2026-06-13).** The images here are **7 genuine public-domain photographs of the original Topkapı cards** (Wikimedia Commons, *Mamluk playing card 1–7*) — but the pack has **~48 surviving cards**, so these 7 are shown as **REPRESENTATIVE stand-ins**: the same court photograph (*card 6*) currently illustrates all four suits' deputies, and the pips reuse one or two images. That is a display stand-in, NOT a claim that the four deputies (or pips) are identical, and the specific image↔suit/rank assignments are **not yet verified against the originals**. TODO: source the full surviving set from Wikimedia Commons *Category: Mamluk playing cards* and the reconstructions at mamluk.spiorad.net / l-pollett.tripod.com, and map each image to its actual card. [@web_commons_mamluk; @web_spiorad_topkapi; @web_pollett_mamluk_cards64]

sections.What it is: A 52-card, four-suit pack (~48 survive — about 43 from the original pack plus a few later replacements) from the Mamluk Sultanate, in the Topkapı Palace Museum. The structure — four suits of ten pips plus three courts — is the skeleton Europe inherited.
sections.Why it matters: Tarot = this pack + a fifth suit of trumps. Remove the 21 triumphs and the Fool from any historical tarot and you are left with a Mamluk-shaped deck.
sections.Provenance: Found and published by L. A. Mayer (1939; monograph 1971). Held at the Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul.
sections.Scene: A hand-painted card from the Mamluk playing card collection (now dispersed in the Topkapi Palace Museum and Benaki Museum). The card shown is from the cups suit, elaborately painted with arabesque scrollwork, intricate geometric borders, and an Arabic inscription panel at top identifying rank.
sections.Symbol: The Mamluk pack is the oldest surviving complete (or near-complete) set of playing cards. Its aniconic design — no human or animal figures — reflects Islamic artistic conventions, encoding rank and value through geometric and floral ornament alone.

sections.What it is: Ten pip cards (Ace–10) plus three courts. Carried into Europe directly as Cups (coppe / coupes).
sections.Scene: Pip and court cards of the cups suit (ṭūmān). Pip cards show the cup symbol — a rounded chalice form — repeated according to the card's numerical value within elegant geometric borders. Court cards bear Arabic titles in calligraphic panels: Malik (king), Nā'ib al-kabīr (upper deputy), Nā'ib al-thānī (lower deputy).
sections.Symbol: The cup (ṭūmān) suit in the Mamluk pack is the direct ancestor of the cups suit in European tarot and Italian playing cards. Its form — a rounded goblet — passed intact through Moorish Spain and Sicily into Christian Europe.

sections.What it is: Ten pips plus three courts. Carried into Europe as Coins (denari / deniers).
sections.Scene: Pip and court cards of the coins suit (darāhim). The coin symbol — a round disc bearing a square or geometric inset — is repeated in the pip cards, arranged in balanced geometric grids. Court card panels bear Arabic calligraphic titles.
sections.Symbol: The coins (darāhim) suit directly prefigures the coins/pentacles suit of tarot. The coin symbol retained its circular form throughout its transmission into European card culture.

sections.What it is: Ten pips plus three courts. The Mamluk swords are straight; carried into Europe as Swords (spade / épées).
sections.Scene: Pip and court cards of the swords suit (suyūf). Pip cards show curved scimitar-like sword forms arranged in interlocking patterns. The swords fan and cross each other in increasingly complex configurations at higher pip values.
sections.Symbol: The swords (suyūf) represent military rank and martial honour in Mamluk culture. The suit passed into Europe as the swords suit, retaining the curved blade of Islamic weaponry before later Italian and Spanish versions straightened the design.

sections.What it is: Ten pips plus three courts. Polo was a Mamluk court sport Europeans did not play — so this suit was redrawn as Batons / Wands (bastoni / bâtons). The clearest single fingerprint of the Islamic origin.
sections.Scene: Pip and court cards of the polo-sticks suit (jawkān). The polo mallet — a long staff with a curved head — is shown in various arrangements and multiples on pip cards. Polo was the premier sport of Mamluk military culture.
sections.Symbol: The polo-stick suit is the only suit that did not survive intact into European cards. When the deck passed to Europe via the Moors, jawkān was transformed into the wands/batons suit, the curved mallet head becoming a straight staff or club.

sections.What it is: Each suit has three court cards: the **malik** (king), the **nā'ib** (viceroy / deputy), and the **thānī nā'ib** (second deputy). Hence the game's name, *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb*, 'Kings and Deputies.'
sections.Aniconic: Following Islamic norms against figural images, the courts show no human figures — only abstract ornament and calligraphic inscriptions (rank names, sometimes verses of courtly praise). Europe later replaced the second deputy with a mounted Knight and added a Queen.
sections.Scene: Three court ranks are distinguished on each Mamluk suit: the Malik (king) shown with a more elaborated border, the Nā'ib al-kabīr (upper viceroy/deputy), and the Nā'ib al-thānī (lower viceroy/deputy). Court cards are distinguished by the Arabic title panel and the elaborateness of their arabesque decoration, not by figurative images of persons.
sections.Symbol: The three-rank Mamluk court (king + two deputies) likely evolved into the four-rank European court (king, queen, knight, page) through the addition of female figures during the passage into Christian Europe.

sections.Card: The king (malik) of the Cups suit. An aniconic court card: no figure, only decorative and calligraphic ornament proclaiming the rank. The three Cups courts (malik, nā'ib, thānī nā'ib) became the King, Knight, and Page of the European Latin-suited packs; the Queen was added later.
sections.Image note: Image confirmed: *Mamluk playing card 7* (Wikimedia Commons) is identified via Commons categories as the King of Cups. One of three confirmed image↔card assignments in this grammar.
sections.Scene: A court card from the cups suit identified as Malik (king) by its Arabic title inscription. The card bears particularly elaborate arabesque decoration in the main field and a formal calligraphic title panel at the top.
sections.Symbol: The Malik of cups: highest ranking, most elaborately decorated card of the suit, encoding authority through ornamental density.

sections.Card: The viceroy (nā'ib) of the Cups suit — the senior deputy court card. Aniconic calligraphic design.
sections.Image note: Shown with a representative surviving court card (Topkapı); the same photo stands in across suits pending per-card sourcing — not a claim that the deputies are identical. See the deck's Image Provenance note.
sections.Scene: A court card from the cups suit, rank Nā'ib (deputy/viceroy). Arabic calligraphic title panel at top; the arabesque decoration is rich but slightly less elaborate than the Malik card.
sections.Symbol: The deputy rank — one or two per suit — is the direct ancestor of the European knight and page cards.

sections.Card: A surviving pip card from the Cups suit. Pip cards carry the suit symbol repeated numerically (Ace through 10) with decorative borders — entirely abstract, no narrative content. The suit symbol here is the cup (ṭūmān).
sections.Image note: Image confirmed: *Mamluk playing card 2* (Wikimedia Commons) is identified via Commons categories as the 3 of Cups.
sections.Scene: A pip card from the cups suit showing the cup symbol — a rounded chalice form — repeated in a geometric arrangement within an arabesque border.
sections.Symbol: The cup pip: pure numerical value communicated through repetition of the suit emblem, a system that survives unchanged in European pip cards.

sections.Card: The king (malik) of the Coins suit. Aniconic court card with calligraphic inscription. Carried into Europe as the King of Coins (denari / deniers).
sections.Scene: A court card from the coins suit, rank Malik (king). Identified by its Arabic title and the richest arabesque decoration in the suit. The coin symbol — a round disc with square inset — appears in the decoration.
sections.Symbol: Material authority and wealth communicated through the abstraction of the coin symbol and the density of its ornamental framing.

sections.Card: The viceroy (nā'ib) of the Coins suit. Aniconic calligraphic design.
sections.Image note: Shown with a representative surviving court card (Topkapı); the same photo stands in across suits pending per-card sourcing — not a claim that the deputies are identical. See the deck's Image Provenance note.
sections.Scene: A court card from the coins suit, rank Nā'ib. Arabesque field with a calligraphic title panel; slightly simpler ornamental scheme than the Malik.
sections.Symbol: Delegated material authority; the viceroy who manages wealth on behalf of the king.

sections.Card: A surviving pip card from the Coins suit. The coin symbol (darāhim) repeated numerically with decorative borders.
sections.Image note: Image confirmed: *Mamluk playing card 1* (Wikimedia Commons) is identified via Commons categories as the 6 of Coins.
sections.Scene: A pip card from the coins suit showing the round disc coin symbol repeated in balanced geometric arrangements within a fine arabesque border.
sections.Symbol: Numerical coin pip: pure quantity expressed as repeating circular forms.

sections.Card: The king (malik) of the Swords suit. Aniconic. Mamluk swords are straight (not the curved scimitar) — this detail carried into European Swords suits which also show straight blades.
sections.Scene: A court card from the swords suit, rank Malik. The Arabic calligraphic title panel identifies it; the arabesque decoration is the most elaborate of the suit. Curved sword forms may appear subtly within the ornamental field.
sections.Symbol: Military kingship encoded through abstraction; the highest rank of martial authority.

sections.Card: The viceroy (nā'ib) of the Swords suit. Aniconic calligraphic design.
sections.Image note: Shown with a representative surviving court card (Topkapı); the same photo stands in across suits pending per-card sourcing — not a claim that the deputies are identical. See the deck's Image Provenance note.
sections.Scene: A court card from the swords suit, rank Nā'ib. Calligraphic title panel with rich but secondary arabesque decoration.
sections.Symbol: Delegated martial authority; the military commander serving the king.

sections.Card: A surviving pip card from the Swords suit. Straight-bladed sword symbol repeated numerically.
sections.Scene: A pip card from the swords suit showing curved scimitar forms arranged in interlocking or fanning patterns, contained within arabesque borders.
sections.Symbol: Martial numerical value expressed through interlocking curved blades.

sections.Card: The king (malik) of the Polo-sticks suit. Aniconic. The jawkān (polo mallet) was a Mamluk court sport marker. Europeans, unfamiliar with polo, redrew this suit as Batons / Wands — the transformation is visible when comparing this card to a Visconti batons card.
sections.Scene: A court card from the polo-sticks suit, rank Malik. The Arabic title identifies it; arabesque decoration is at its richest for this suit. The polo mallet form may appear within the ornamental field.
sections.Symbol: The prestige of polo as the sport of the Mamluk military elite is encoded in this highest-ranking card of the jawkān suit.

sections.Card: The viceroy (nā'ib) of the Polo-sticks suit. Aniconic calligraphic design.
sections.Image note: Shown with a representative surviving court card (Topkapı); the same photo stands in across suits pending per-card sourcing — not a claim that the deputies are identical. See the deck's Image Provenance note.
sections.Scene: A court card from the polo-sticks suit, rank Nā'ib. Calligraphic title panel; secondary level of arabesque decoration.
sections.Symbol: Delegated authority in the sphere of sport and martial recreation.

sections.Card: A surviving pip card from the Polo-sticks suit. The jawkān symbol repeated numerically. When this suit crossed to Europe as Batons / Wands, the stick shape was retained but the polo context was lost.
sections.Scene: A pip card from the polo-sticks suit showing the polo mallet (a long staff with curved head) repeated in geometric arrangements within arabesque borders.
sections.Symbol: The jawkān pip: the suit symbol that became the wands/batons of European tarot when the curved mallet head was straightened into a staff.
